Adventure 4. No use crying over stolen milk. Alright, I began as soon as I walked in the door the next day. I'd been up for hours the night before thinking about the challenge. He's not somehow siphoning off the milk on the way to the customer. Tom, who'd been reading the newspaper, didn't seem surprised by my eagerness. Nope, the detectives would have seen him. No hanky-panky at the customer sites? No, the detectives would have seen that as well. He's not bribing the customer's workers to steal it for him? Nope, he's on his own. I sighed, defeated. Then I give up. Let me give you a hint. You're not thinking backwards enough. Well, that's the first time I've ever been told off for not thinking backwards. You see, often we try to solve problems and come up with ideas that apply to only a limited time frame, such as the time from when the driver arrives to have his truck filled up at the dairy farm. But this fellow was clever. He must have known about the seventh box. Do it beforehand. Aha! So he rigged up some way of stealing the milk before he arrived to pick it up. Exactly. Can you figure out how? I thought for a while. No, I said finally. How do you steal something before you even see it? Tricky, eh? Here's how. Before the driver set out for the dairy farm, he took a few large buckets and hung them inside the truck. That way, when the dairy workers filled the truck with milk, the buckets got filled as well. But when the tank was emptied at the processing plant, the buckets stayed full, and the trucker was able to reach in, pull it out, empty them at his leisure, and milk some profits by selling fresh milk to friends and family. This box, do it beforehand, encourages you to focus on what you can do earlier that will help you later. It's extraordinarily useful because, as I said, people are often so focused on the problem itself and what happens when the problem occurs that they don't see what can be done before the issue rears its ugly head. Same goes for opportunities. People focus so much on what can be done during a certain window of time. The time a machine is in operation, or the time a customer is in the store, that they don't take advantage of what could be done before the machine does its job, or before the customer walks in the door. Tom reached under his work table and pulled out what looked like a piece of wood stained to a fiery red, clearly switching gears yet again. Like it, he asked. Beautiful, I replied, running my hands across the grain. Nice even stain job. Nope. That wood is unstained. Painted? I ventured. Nope. Unpainted. Wow. I've never seen trees naturally produce such a deep red color. Neither have I, because there's nothing natural about it. I'll give you a hint. We do it beforehand. You stain the wood beforehand? Yep. I'm developing a system to send pigment to the roots, which then gets absorbed by the tree. Or to put it a little differently, we water the trees with tinted water. Huh. I never would have thought to do that. You would, if you looked in this do it beforehand box. I'm also working with a medical supply company on a better way to take casts off broken limbs once they've healed without damaging the skin or scaring patients half to death by chiseling or sawing away toward their skin. Got any ideas? I'm guessing it won't have anything to do with what's done to the cast when it's ready to come off, but rather before it's put on. Exactly. I found a way to embed a small saw blade into the cast. When it's ready to come off, the doctor hooks a handle to holes on each end of the blade and just starts cutting outward from within away from the skin. I shook my head in disbelief. This guy really was full of great ideas. Now as for your bookstore, pick a problem and let's try to solve it inside this box. Okay, I began slowly. I'd like people to buy more books when they come in the store. But until now, I've only looked at what I could do to sell them more after they walk in the door. What could I do before they come in? I pondered this carefully, looking around Tom's workshop for inspiration. Just as I was about to give up, I noticed a stack of books sitting on his desk. Boom, I had an idea. Hmm, how about this? It seems like most people spend half their time in the store looking for books. What if they could call or message before they come in, give us a list of the types of books or subject they might be interested in, and we'd have a stack of books waiting for them when they arrive. That's a great idea, said Tom. I'd certainly want to do that myself instead of spending so much of my time figuring out whether something I want is shelved under physics, science or something else entirely. And I'd probably wind up buying more books than if I'd had to search for them myself. I guess I really need to spend some time in this box, I said, because it occurs to me that in both my marketing and in my running the bookstore, I've been ignoring the beforehand and focusing instead on what's right at hand. I need to take a good, hard look at things like planning and training my people before they start working with customers. You're really starting to get the idea here, said Tom. And look, these boxes are just starting points. You never know what you may find in them, and there's no right or wrong. For example, another trick I use is to think of boxes in terms of their opposites. So do it beforehand might make you think of things you don't do or need to stop doing beforehand. I can see where I'm going to be able to solve a lot of my problems with these boxes, I said. But I've been wanting to ask you, aren't there just some things you can't do anything about? Like, aren't some things just going to happen no matter what you do? That's a good point, said Tom, seemingly glad I had brought it up. And there's a box for those tricksters as well. The trash box? No, no, the convert into a benefit box. It's for things you can't or don't want to do anything about. Very often, you can turn them into a benefit if you look at them a little more closely, or a little differently. You accept that the problem, or so-called negative outcome, is going to happen. But instead of seeing what you can do beforehand to prevent it, you see what you can do after to turn it into a benefit. It's the old lemons into lemonade trick. But it's amazing how rarely most people actually employ this technique. For example, we used to have a problem running acid through the pipes in a factory where I worked. It would eat the heck out of the pipes interior in no time. Interestingly, we also discovered that when we pumped alkaline or base substances through the pipes, it left a sediment. This was a problem as well, because the pipes would become clogged. So we came up with the clever solution of alternating between pumping acid and base liquids through the pipes, converting both negatives into a positive. Genius, I exclaimed. Tom barely even paused before launching into the next story. He had a way of shifting subjects quickly and keeping you on your toes. And have you ever seen the sea lions at Fisherman's Wharf in San Francisco? He asked. Yes, it's an amazing sight. I recalled my trip to the wharf years before, where I'd witnessed hundreds of sea lions sunning themselves, playing, fighting, and making a racket. And a great tourist attraction. Right. But they weren't always seen as such an asset. The story is that one day a few seals started showing up and sunning themselves on some floating docks next to Fisherman's Wharf. Before long, their friends and relatives joined them, and soon there was a huge number of seals and nothing that could be done about it. There was no way to evict the seals from their new home. Although seals got to be a real problem. They were loud. They were aggressive. They were messy. The boat owners couldn't get to their boats. And more and more were showing up every day. So, folks started coming up with all sorts of plans to get rid of the seals. Some proposed capturing them and relocating them very far away. Jacques Cousteau himself offered to rig up a diver and a shark fin to scare them off. Some of the store owners on the pier were probably ready to just shoot them. But then someone noticed that all the tourists actually loved watching the sea lions. The person then had the idea to stop trying to deal with the problem and just leave the sea lions alone and turn them into a benefit. The wharf began promoting the sea lions as a reason to come to Fisherman's Wharf. And that collection of tacky seafood shacks and souvenir stands became more popular than ever. And remember the driver was stealing the milk? Tom asked, suddenly shifting topics yet again. Sure, I replied. Are you going to tell me how to turn theft into a benefit? In a way, replied Tom. The rest of the story is that instead of agonizing over all the money they had lost on stolen milk, private detectives and unhappy customers, and having to find a new driver, I added. Yes. And instead of feeling sorry for themselves, they turned it into a benefit. Can you figure out how? You said the driver was stealing it to sell to his family and friends. Maybe the dairy, instead of selling all their milk to the big processor, should bottle some of it themselves and sell directly if there's so much demand. Right you are, agreed Tom. Sometimes what seems like a problem or negative is just a piece of machinery or a system or, in business, a market trying to reveal an opportunity to us. They wound up setting up a nice little, highly profitable side business, bottling some of their milk themselves and selling it at a discount to their employees and their friends and families. Now let's toss some of your problems or negative outcomes into this turn it into a benefit box and see what happens. How about returns? I suggested. I hate it when people return books and want their money back. I know it's their right to do so, according to our policy, but I can honestly just feel that money coming right out of my pocket. Sometimes, off for Tom, you can turn a negative into a benefit by exaggerating it. What do you mean? I asked. I should encourage people to return books? Take sand and gravel, Tom said, making another one of his unexpected shifts. In very cold climate, sand or gravel often stick together in transit and become difficult to unload. But if you super freeze the mixture by adding some liquid nitrogen, the sand or gravel goes from hard to brittle, breaks up and becomes a breeze to unload. Just try doing the same with your returns problem and see where it takes you. Alright? I'll encourage them to return books, I said, almost sarcastically at first, and then, before I knew it, the ideas had started flowing. Return all you want, in any condition. Take 30 days. Heck, take a couple of months. Take a year. What do I care? At this point, I felt like I was getting carried away, but the smile on Tom's face encouraged me to continue. Hey, you know, I could say all that, but very few people, if any, would really take advantage of it. And meanwhile, it would certainly make people more comfortable about buying books in the first place. And it would also make me stand out from the chain book stores with all their strict corporate policies of no returns after 30 days and books must be returned in original condition. Whoa, I wonder if I can do this with all my store's problems? I blurted out, my head now swimming. Let's see, negative. Too many teenagers hanging out in the store. Maybe we could try offering special events built around books that teens would actually be interested Let's turn them into customers. Hmm. Another negative might be not enough traffic in the store. Why should they have to come to the store? Why not start a program that delivers books to people's homes or businesses? I could hire an intern to deliver books or contract with a delivery service. What else? Let's see. Uh, well, not being able to offer customers as wide a selection as the big chain book stores. How can I turn this into a benefit? I stopped to think for a moment. It means fewer books for people to have to look through to find what they want. Perhaps I should actually promote the fact that we carry only the very best of the best. We've carefully curated our books so you don't have to wade through a mountain of them to find the ones you really want. That's the idea. Encouraged Tom. Wait, I've got more problems. Oh, well, I guess I can solve them anytime now that I know about these boxes. This has been an amazing lesson and to think that it all started with a thief. Ah, Tom responded dramatically. That brings me to another of the great secrets of problem solving and creativity. The best, most original ideas often come from outside your field of expertise. That's because the ideas that come from our own fields are often polluted with inbred ways of looking at things and false notions of what will and what won't work. Instead, the trick is to look inside boxes from other fields and other industries from people who don't think and look at the world the same way you do. That's why so many breakthrough inventions were made by dabblers. People who tackled and solved a so-called impossible problem in a field not their own because they brought a fresh perspective. Alexander Graham Bell was a teacher of the deaf who dabbled in electronics when he invented the telephone. The Wright brothers were bicycle makers who had no business dabbling in the science of aerodynamics. And as a very young man, Philo Farnsworth came up with the idea that made television possible as he mowed hay on a farm in neat rows and realized that an electron beam could scan an image in horizontal lines. So here's your homework for next time. To give yourself an outsider's perspective. First, I want you to think of two numbers between one and four. Rather puzzled, I jutted my lip and replied two and four? Good. Next, I want you to go to the magazine rack in your bookstore. When you get there, go to the second row and take the fourth magazine from the right. If it's publishers weekly, put it back and try again until you find something in a field totally unrelated to any of your interests. Hopefully it will be something like doll collecting monthly. Read it and see what you can learn that you can apply to your own business. In fact, why don't we meet next time at your bookstore. Then I can see how your operations work in real time and you can tell me all about what you've learned.
